questions.question: In an NFA, what does the transition function δ(q, a) return?
questions.explanation: Unlike a DFA where δ returns a single state, an NFA's δ(q, a) returns a set of states (an element of the power set P(Q)). This is what allows non-deterministic branching.

questions.question: What does ε-closure(q) represent?
options: The set of all states reachable from q using exactly one ε-transition
The set of all states reachable from q using zero or more ε-transitions
The set of all accepting states in the NFA
The set of all states that can reach q via any transition
questions.explanation: ε-closure(q) is the set of all states reachable from q by following zero or more ε-transitions. It always includes q itself (zero ε-transitions). This is fundamental to NFA simulation and subset construction.

questions.question: If an NFA has n states, what is the maximum number of states in the DFA produced by subset construction?
questions.explanation: Each DFA state corresponds to a subset of NFA states. Since there are 2ⁿ possible subsets of an n-element set, the DFA can have at most 2ⁿ states. This is the exponential blowup — worst case, though rarely achieved in practice.

questions.question: An NFA has states {q0, q1}, alphabet {a}, transitions: δ(q0, a) = {q0, q1}, δ(q1, a) = ∅, start = q0, accepting = {q1}. After subset construction, how many states does the resulting DFA have?
questions.explanation: The reachable subsets are: {q0} (start), {q0, q1} (from {q0} on 'a'), and {q1} would only appear if we could reach it alone — but from {q0, q1} on 'a' we get {q0, q1} again (since δ(q0, a) = {q0, q1} and δ(q1, a) = ∅). Actually: {q0} → 'a' → {q0, q1}, and {q0, q1} → 'a' → {q0, q1}. So the DFA has 2 states: {q0} and {q0, q1}. The accepting DFA state is {q0, q1} since it contains q1.

questions.question: What does the subset construction (powerset construction) prove?
options: That NFAs are more powerful than DFAs
That every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA
That regular expressions cannot describe all regular languages
questions.explanation: The subset construction converts any NFA to an equivalent DFA by creating states that represent sets of NFA states. The resulting DFA may have exponentially many states (up to 2^n for n NFA states), but it recognizes exactly the same language.
